title: The operating system - the manager in the middle
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: The operating system, with its kernel at the core, manages the hardware and shares it between programs. A program asks the OS to do anything privileged - open a file, get memory, talk to the network - through a system call.
explanation: Your program never really touches the disk or the network itself. The operating system stands in the middle, and its core - the kernel - is the only code allowed to drive the hardware directly. When your program needs something real, it makes a system call: a controlled request across the line into the kernel ('open this file', 'give me memory', 'send these bytes'). This is what keeps one program from trampling another or crashing the machine - the OS mediates everything and decides who gets what.

exercises.question: The core of the OS that's allowed to drive the hardware directly is the...
A program asks the OS to do something privileged through a...
hints: A request across the line into the kernel.

exercises.question: Why can't your program just write to the disk hardware itself?
options: the OS mediates hardware so programs don't trample each other
hints: Think about many programs sharing one machine.
Something has to keep order and safety.
The OS mediates access to the hardware.
